refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output (energy_per_ton) to support evaluation against a customer-defined energy efficiency target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the capability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a performance target. The refined FR focuses on the functional requirement—enabling visibility and traceability of energy efficiency data—which is solution-neutral and within system control. Achievement of the target (E_target ≤ energy_per_ton) is a performance acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement. This separation clarifies that the system must provide the measurement infrastructure; actual target achievement depends on design choices (DP/IM) and operational conditions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution collects, calculates, and reports energy_per_ton metrics (energy_used / ton_moved) with defined accuracy and temporal granularity over the agreed evaluation period, independent of whether the reported value meets the customer's target threshold.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed by the system during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure or infer the quantity of work output (tons moved) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall calculate energy_per_ton as the ratio of energy consumed to work output.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall record and report energy_per_ton metrics with defined temporal resolution and accuracy.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument the energy source (fuel/power input) with continuous measurement; instrument the work output (tonnage moved) with continuous measurement; compute energy_per_ton as a derived metric in real-time and store historical records for periodic reporting against E_target.

DP2: Capture discrete energy consumption events (refueling, battery charge cycles) and discrete work completion events (load delivered, distance traveled); correlate events post-hoc to compute energy_per_ton over agreed evaluation windows; report aggregated results.

DP3: Establish a baseline energy_per_ton under controlled reference conditions; measure deviations from baseline during operational use; report cumulative efficiency variance against E_target as a proxy for absolute energy_per_ton.

DP4: Delegate energy and tonnage measurement to independent third-party monitoring service; provide standardized data feeds (timestamps, energy units, tonnage units) to external system; receive certified energy_per_ton reports for customer comparison against E_target.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Instrument the energy source (fuel/power input) with continuous measurement; instrument the work output (tonnage moved) with continuous measurement; compute energy_per_ton as a derived metric in real-time and store historical records for periodic reporting against E_target.

potential_sources: Energy measurement and tonnage measurement are logically independent (different physical phenomena, different sensor domains); coupling risk is low if measurement subsystems are decoupled via standardized data interfaces.

Real-time computation and historical storage are separable concerns; risk of tight coupling if computation logic is embedded in storage layer—mitigated by explicit separation of compute and persistence modules.
Reporting logic (aggregation, filtering, comparison to E_target) should be decoupled from measurement acquisition to allow independent evolution of reporting rules without sensor recalibration.
Acceptance criteria explicitly require energy_used and ton_moved as independent inputs; DP1 respects this separation.
coupling_check.notes: DP1 exhibits low inherent coupling because it decomposes into three quasi-independent concerns: (1) energy measurement, (2) tonnage measurement, (3) metric derivation and reporting. Each can be realized by separate implementation modules with well-defined interfaces. Risk of coupling arises only if modules share state or synchronization assumptions; this is manageable through interface discipline.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy source and work output are measurable with sufficient accuracy and temporal resolution to support meaningful energy_per_ton computation. Risk: If measurement noise or granularity is coarse, derived metric may not discriminate efficiency variations; mitigation: specify measurement accuracy requirements (e.g., ±5% energy, ±2% tonnage) early.

Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough to average transient effects and operational variability. Risk: Short evaluation windows may yield energy_per_ton values that do not reflect steady-state efficiency; mitigation: align evaluation period with customer operational cycles and worksite conditions.

Assumption: Energy and tonnage measurements are synchronized in time (or can be correlated post-hoc). Risk: Asynchronous or delayed measurements may introduce systematic bias in energy_per_ton; mitigation: establish clock synchronization and latency budgets for measurement subsystems.

Assumption: E_target is a fixed, well-defined threshold provided by customer. Risk: If E_target is ambiguous, context-dependent, or subject to change, reporting against it becomes unreliable; mitigation: require explicit, signed acceptance criteria document before deployment.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not prohibit continuous measurement or data logging. Risk: Safety or privacy constraints may restrict sensor placement or data retention; mitigation: conduct early safety and compliance review with customer and site operators.

Assumption: Historical record storage and retrieval are reliable and auditable. Risk: Data loss, corruption, or tampering could invalidate energy_per_ton reports; mitigation: implement data integrity checks (checksums, immutable logs) and define retention/archival policy.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable continuous measurement of energy consumption (energy_used, in standardized units) and work output (ton_moved, in standardized units) per agreed evaluation period, compute the derived metric energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved in real-time, store historical records with timestamps and data integrity metadata, and generate periodic reports comparing measured energy_per_ton against a customer-defined energy efficiency target (E_target) to support evaluation of compliance. The solution shall maintain measurement accuracy within ±5% for energy and ±2% for tonnage, synchronize measurements to within ±10 seconds, and preserve audit trails for all reported values. Acceptance criteria: Measured energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over the agreed evaluation period, with documented measurement uncertainty and operational context (e.g., load type, terrain, ambient conditions) included in each report.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized energy efficiency monitoring and reporting system that owns instrumentation logic, metric computation, historical storage, and periodic reporting against energy targets across all vehicle operations.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives continuous fuel/power input measurements and tonnage/work output data from Vehicle instrumentation points

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits computed energy_per_ton derived metrics and historical efficiency records for display and analysis

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives E_target threshold values and reporting schedule parameters from operational planning

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Optionally signals when energy_per_ton deviates significantly from E_target (indicating potential equipment malfunction or safety concern)

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of fleet-wide energy efficiency analytics, metric derivation, and historical reporting. Instrumentation data flows in via Telemetry; targets and schedules flow in via Tasking. Minimal diffusion: only the raw sensor signals must originate in Vehicle; all computation, storage, and reporting logic remains contained. This is a system-level analytics function, not a vehicle-level function.

Poor fit: Vehicle would own only the raw instrumentation (fuel/power sensors, tonnage sensors) but NOT the metric computation, storage, or reporting logic. The DP explicitly requires real-time computation and historical record storage—these are fleet-level concerns that would diffuse into NGHE Solution anyway. Placing computation logic in Vehicle creates tight coupling and duplicates logic across multiple vehicle instances.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is concerned with remote control and situational awareness for operators, not energy efficiency analytics. Energy metric computation and historical reporting have no natural home in Teleoperation and would be highly diffuse. This violates separation of concerns.

Weak fit: Safety Services might consume energy_per_ton as a diagnostic signal (e.g., anomalous efficiency could indicate equipment failure), but it should not own the computation or storage of the metric itself. Energy efficiency is an operational/business metric, not a safety function. Placing the DP here conflates safety monitoring with efficiency monitoring.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP is fundamentally a fleet-level analytics and reporting function. It requires continuous aggregation of data from multiple vehicles, centralized computation of derived metrics, persistent storage of historical records, and periodic comparison against organizational targets. These are all characteristics of a system-level service, not a vehicle-level or operator-level service. NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner because it is the system-level analytics and optimization platform. Vehicle provides only the raw instrumentation; NGHE Solution provides the intelligence layer.
containment_risks: Latency: If real-time computation is required for operator feedback, network latency between Vehicle and NGHE Solution could introduce unacceptable delay. Mitigation: clarify whether 'real-time' means sub-second or periodic (e.g., every 10 seconds). If sub-second is required, consider a hybrid approach where Vehicle performs local buffering and NGHE Solution performs aggregation.
Data loss: If Vehicle-to-NGHE communication is interrupted, historical records may be incomplete. Mitigation: implement local buffering in Vehicle with eventual consistency reconciliation.

Coupling to Telemetry interface: The DP depends heavily on the Telemetry interface schema. Changes to sensor definitions or data formats will ripple into NGHE Solution. Mitigation: define a stable, versioned Telemetry contract.

Scalability: As fleet size grows, NGHE Solution must scale to handle metric computation and storage for many vehicles. Mitigation: design NGHE Solution with horizontal scalability (e.g., distributed time-series database, stream processing).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP is recommended. The DP is well-formed and logically sound. However, clarify the real-time requirement: if 'real-time' means sub-second feedback to operators, consider revising the DP to specify that Vehicle performs local energy_per_ton computation (for immediate feedback) while NGHE Solution performs aggregation and historical reporting (for fleet-level analytics). This would split the DP into two: DP1a (local efficiency feedback in Vehicle) and DP1b (fleet-level efficiency analytics in NGHE Solution). For now, assume 'real-time' means periodic (e.g., every reporting interval) and keep the DP as stated, with NGHE Solution as the sole owner.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 directly and completely addresses the FR. The DP specifies three essential mechanisms: (1) continuous measurement of energy source (fuel/power input), (2) continuous measurement of work output (tonnage), and (3) real-time computation of energy_per_ton with historical storage for periodic reporting against E_target. This logically satisfies the FR requirement to 'enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output to support evaluation against a customer-defined energy efficiency target.' The DP is solution-neutral, measurable, and decomposable.

diffusion_risks: Latency diffusion: If 'real-time' computation is interpreted as sub-second feedback to operators, the network latency between Vehicle instrumentation and NGHE Solution could introduce unacceptable delay. This would force computation logic to diffuse into Vehicle, creating a hybrid DP that is not clearly stated. Mitigation: explicitly define 'real-time' as periodic (e.g., 10-second intervals) or accept local Vehicle-level buffering with eventual consistency.

Data loss diffusion: Interruption of Vehicle-to-NGHE communication could cause incomplete historical records. This risk may force local buffering logic into Vehicle, diffusing the DP across two modules. Mitigation: implement explicit local buffering in Vehicle with reconciliation protocol, and document this as a secondary IM boundary.

Telemetry interface coupling: The DP depends on stable, versioned Telemetry contracts for fuel/power and tonnage data. Schema changes will ripple into NGHE Solution metric computation. Mitigation: establish a frozen Telemetry interface specification before DP implementation.

Scalability diffusion: As fleet size grows, NGHE Solution must horizontally scale metric computation and storage. If scalability is not architected upfront, computation logic may diffuse across multiple NGHE instances or edge nodes. Mitigation: design NGHE Solution with distributed time-series storage and stream processing from the outset.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct and natural owner of this DP (containment_score 0.92, diffusion_score 0.08). The DP is fundamentally a fleet-level analytics and reporting function that requires centralized aggregation, derived metric computation, persistent historical storage, and periodic comparison against organizational targets. Vehicle provides only raw instrumentation (fuel/power sensors, tonnage sensors); NGHE Solution provides the intelligence layer. The external interfaces are well-defined: Telemetry (in) for sensor data, Tasking (in) for E_target and reporting schedules, Telemetry (out) for computed metrics, and SafetyAlert (out) for anomaly signaling. No other module (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) is a suitable owner. The IM boundary is clear and defensible.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion 'Measured energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved satisfies ≤ E_target over the agreed evaluation period' is directly verifiable against NGHE Solution's historical records and reporting output. The metric is quantitative, traceable to raw sensor inputs (fuel/power and tonnage), and comparable to a customer-defined threshold. Verification requires: (1) audit trail of raw sensor data, (2) audit trail of metric computation (energy_per_ton derivation), (3) historical records stored in NGHE Solution, and (4) periodic reports generated by NGHE Solution. All of these are owned by the NGHE Solution IM boundary and are within its control.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: High. The FR is allocated to a single, well-scoped IM boundary (NGHE Solution) with clear ownership of all required functions: instrumentation data ingestion, metric computation, historical storage, and reporting. The external interfaces (Telemetry in/out, Tasking in, SafetyAlert out) are explicit and non-ambiguous. There is no allocation ambiguity or split ownership. Vehicle's role is limited to providing raw sensor signals; NGHE Solution's role is to transform those signals into actionable efficiency metrics and historical records. This allocation is consistent with the worksite safety plan and operational policies, which assume a centralized fleet management system (NGHE Solution) that monitors and reports on vehicle performance.

recommended_revisions: Clarify the 'real-time' requirement in DP1: specify whether real-time means sub-second feedback (< 1 second latency) or periodic reporting (e.g., every 10 seconds or every reporting interval). If sub-second is required, revise DP1 to include a hybrid approach: Vehicle performs local energy_per_ton buffering for immediate operator feedback, while NGHE Solution performs fleet-level aggregation and historical reporting. This would split DP1 into DP1a (local efficiency feedback) and DP1b (fleet-level efficiency analytics).

Add explicit data loss and reconciliation requirements to DP1: specify that Vehicle maintains local buffering of fuel/power and tonnage measurements during communication outages, and that NGHE Solution implements eventual consistency reconciliation when communication is restored. This mitigates the diffusion risk of incomplete historical records.

Establish a frozen Telemetry interface specification before DP implementation: define the schema, units, sampling rate, and versioning strategy for fuel/power input and tonnage output measurements. This prevents schema changes from rippling into NGHE Solution metric computation logic.

Design NGHE Solution for horizontal scalability: specify that metric computation and historical storage use distributed time-series databases (e.g., InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) and stream processing frameworks (e.g., Kafka, Flink) to handle fleet growth without diffusing computation logic across multiple instances.

Document the SafetyAlert interface contract: specify the threshold for 'significant deviation' from E_target that triggers a safety alert, and clarify whether SafetyAlert is a diagnostic signal (equipment malfunction) or an operational signal (efficiency degradation). This prevents Safety Services from conflating safety monitoring with efficiency monitoring.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP is logically sound, the IM boundary (NGHE Solution) is correct and defensible, and verification is feasible. However, the recommended revisions expose critical architectural decisions that must be resolved before detailed design: (1) the interpretation of 'real-time' (sub-second vs. periodic) determines whether computation diffuses into Vehicle; (2) data loss and reconciliation strategies must be explicit to prevent incomplete historical records; (3) the Telemetry interface contract must be frozen to prevent schema ripple; (4) scalability architecture must be designed upfront to prevent diffusion across multiple NGHE instances; (5) the SafetyAlert interface must be clarified to prevent conflation of safety and efficiency monitoring. These are not defects in the current FR-DP-IM mapping, but rather second-order design decisions that emerge from the diffusion risk analysis. Decomposing into sub-FRs will force these decisions to be made explicitly and will create accountability for their resolution. Given the high safety criticality of this system, explicit sub-level FRs are justified to ensure that latency, data integrity, interface stability, scalability, and safety semantics are all addressed as first-class requirements rather than post-hoc mitigations.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Enable real-time (periodic) energy efficiency feedback to operators with defined latency bounds (e.g., ≤ 10 seconds from sensor measurement to operator display) to support immediate awareness of efficiency performance without requiring sub-second responsiveness.

FR1.2: Maintain continuous historical records of energy consumption and work output with guaranteed data integrity and eventual consistency reconciliation across Vehicle-to-NGHE communication outages to support audit-trail compliance and accurate periodic reporting.

FR1.3: Provide a stable, versioned Telemetry interface contract (schema, units, sampling rate, versioning strategy) for fuel/power input and tonnage output measurements to prevent schema changes from rippling into NGHE Solution metric computation logic.

FR1.4: Enable horizontal scalability of energy efficiency metric computation and historical storage to support fleet growth (e.g., 100s to 1000s of vehicles) without diffusing computation logic across multiple NGHE instances or edge nodes.

FR1.5: Distinguish and clearly signal energy efficiency degradation (operational concern) from equipment malfunction or safety anomaly (safety concern) via explicit SafetyAlert interface semantics to prevent Safety Services from conflating efficiency monitoring with safety monitoring.

revise.why: DP1 is logically correct but under-specifies critical architectural decisions that the diffusion risk analysis has exposed. Revise DP1 to explicitly address: (1) the latency model for 'real-time' computation (periodic vs. sub-second, with implications for Vehicle-level buffering); (2) the data loss and reconciliation strategy (local Vehicle buffering with eventual consistency); (3) the Telemetry interface contract (frozen schema, units, sampling rate); (4) the scalability architecture (distributed time-series storage, stream processing); (5) the SafetyAlert semantics (efficiency vs. safety vs. malfunction). These revisions will clarify the DP and reduce the risk of diffusion during implementation. Alternatively, accept DP1 as-is and decompose into sub-DPs at the next level that address each of these concerns explicitly.

assumptions: NGHE Solution has reliable network connectivity to Vehicle instrumentation; communication outages are transient and recoverable within acceptable time windows (e.g., < 1 hour).
Vehicle instrumentation provides stable, calibrated sensor signals for fuel/power input and tonnage output; sensor drift and calibration errors are managed by Vehicle subsystem, not NGHE Solution.
Telemetry interface schema is stable and versioned; breaking changes are coordinated across Vehicle and NGHE Solution teams.
NGHE Solution has sufficient computational and storage capacity to handle fleet-level metric aggregation and historical retention; scalability is architected upfront (not retrofitted).
SafetyAlert interface is distinct from Telemetry and Tasking interfaces; Safety Services does not conflate efficiency monitoring with safety monitoring.
E_target is provided by customer via Tasking interface and is stable over the evaluation period; changes to E_target are tracked and reported separately.

Energy efficiency is a non-safety-critical operational metric; degradation in efficiency does not directly trigger safety-critical actions (e.g., vehicle shutdown). SafetyAlert is informational, not safety-critical.
open_risks: Latency diffusion: If 'real-time' is interpreted as sub-second feedback (< 1 second), Vehicle instrumentation may need to perform local energy_per_ton buffering and computation, diffusing the DP across Vehicle and NGHE Solution. Mitigation: explicitly define 'real-time' as periodic (e.g., 10-second intervals) in FR1.1 and accept eventual consistency.

Data loss diffusion: Communication outages between Vehicle and NGHE Solution could cause incomplete historical records. Mitigation: implement explicit local buffering in Vehicle with reconciliation protocol (FR1.2) and document as secondary IM boundary.

Telemetry interface coupling: Schema changes in Telemetry interface will ripple into NGHE Solution metric computation. Mitigation: establish frozen Telemetry interface specification (FR1.3) before DP implementation.

Scalability diffusion: As fleet size grows, NGHE Solution must horizontally scale metric computation and storage. If scalability is not architected upfront, computation logic may diffuse across multiple NGHE instances or edge nodes. Mitigation: design NGHE Solution with distributed time-series storage and stream processing (FR1.4).

SafetyAlert semantics ambiguity: Unclear whether SafetyAlert signals equipment malfunction (safety concern) or efficiency degradation (operational concern). Mitigation: clarify SafetyAlert interface contract (FR1.5) and prevent Safety Services from conflating efficiency monitoring with safety monitoring.

High safety criticality: This system operates in a high-safety-criticality environment (worksite safety plan). Energy efficiency monitoring must not interfere with or degrade safety-critical functions (e.g., vehicle control, emergency stop, collision avoidance). Mitigation: ensure NGHE Solution is non-safety-critical and does not have control authority over Vehicle; SafetyAlert is informational only.
evidence_needed: Latency requirements: Obtain explicit latency bounds for 'real-time' feedback from customer and operations team. Specify whether sub-second (< 1 second) or periodic (e.g., 10 seconds) is acceptable.
Data loss tolerance: Obtain acceptable data loss thresholds from customer and compliance team. Specify maximum duration of communication outage before historical records are considered incomplete.
Telemetry interface specification: Obtain frozen Telemetry interface schema, units, sampling rate, and versioning strategy from Vehicle team. Document as a design constraint for NGHE Solution.
Scalability targets: Obtain fleet size projections and growth rates from operations team. Specify scalability targets (e.g., support 1000 vehicles, 100 Hz sampling rate) for NGHE Solution design.

SafetyAlert semantics: Obtain clarification from Safety Services and operations team on the distinction between efficiency degradation (operational) and equipment malfunction (safety). Document SafetyAlert threshold and semantics in interface contract.

Safety criticality assessment: Conduct formal safety analysis (FMEA, FTA) to confirm that NGHE Solution is non-safety-critical and does not introduce new failure modes or hazards. Verify that SafetyAlert does not interfere with safety-critical functions.
